label: quisumbing::lagundi_vitex
text: Lagundi (Vitex negundo Linn.) — Family: Verbenaceae/Lamiaceae. From Quisumbing, Medicinal Plants of the Philippines (1951, revised 1978), p.812-816. Filipino names: Lagundi (Tagalog, Bisaya); Dangla (Ilocano); Tugas-tugas (Cebuano). Distribution: Throughout the Philippines, also India, China, tropical Africa. Description: Shrub or small tree 2-8m. Leaves: 5-foliate, opposite, leaflets 5 or 3, lanceolate, tomentose beneath. Flowers: Small, bluish-purple, in panicles. Medicinal uses documented by Quisumbing: Leaves — boiled and used for baths or poultice for skin diseases, rheumatism; decoction taken internally for asthma, cough, fever; fresh leaves applied to forehead for headache. Seeds — used for nervousness. Roots — used for fever and rheumatism. Active compounds identified by Quisumbing era and subsequent research: Essential oil (sabinene, 1,8-cineole, beta-caryophyllene), iridoid glycosides (agnuside, aucubin), flavonoids (casticin, luteolin). DOH registration 1997 as official herbal medicine for cough and asthma. Commercial tablet (Ascof) approved. RCT evidence: multiple Philippine clinical trials.

label: quisumbing::sambong_blumea
text: Sambong (Blumea balsamifera (Linn.) DC.) — Family: Compositae/Asteraceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.493-498. Filipino names: Sambong (Tagalog); Subusob (Ilocano); Bukadkad (Bisaya); Gabon (Pampanga). Distribution: Common throughout the Philippines, India to Japan, tropical Africa. Medicinal uses from Quisumbing: Leaves applied as poultice for rheumatism and swellings. Decoction of leaves taken for fever, diarrhea, and intestinal gas. Leaf decoction used as urinary disinfectant. Dried leaves as herbal tea for kidney stones. Post-Quisumbing validation: DOH registered as herbal medicine 1997 for urolithiasis (kidney stones). Active compound: Blumea camphor (l-borneol) — demonstrated diuretic activity, promotes passage of small kidney stones. Multiple Philippine clinical trials confirming urolithic effect. Also: NGFI-B binding activity (nuclear receptor modulation) discovered — possible applications in metabolic disease. Used across Southeast Asia (Thailand, Indonesia, Vietnam) for overlapping conditions — internal convergence within the region.

label: quisumbing::tsaang_gubat_ehretia
text: Tsaang Gubat (Ehretia microphylla Lam. / Carmona retusa) — Family: Boraginaceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.621-622. Filipino names: Tsaang gubat (Tagalog, meaning 'wild tea'); Marm-ot (Ilocano); Alibubut (Bisaya). Distribution: Lowland forests throughout Philippines. Traditional use documented by Quisumbing: Leaves boiled as a tea substitute; decoction for diarrhea, stomach ache, intestinal spasm, and bathing for skin allergies. DOH registration 1997 for diarrhea. Active research post-Quisumbing: Rutin (flavonol) — antispasmodic mechanism confirmed in animal studies; reduces gut motility. Antibacterial activity against E. coli and Salmonella — validates traditional use for bacterial diarrhea. Multiple Philippine studies confirming antidiarrheal activity in both animal models and limited clinical trials. Used in the Philippines as both daily tea (Carmona retusa leaves) and specific treatment — demonstrating food-medicine continuum typical of traditional pharmacopeia.

label: quisumbing::niyog_niyogan_quisqualis
text: Niyog-niyogan (Quisqualis indica Linn.) — Family: Combretaceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.267-269. Filipino names: Niyog-niyogan (Tagalog, meaning 'Chinese lantern'); Tartarau (Ilocano); Kasumpangi (Bisaya). Distribution: Throughout the Philippines, native to tropical Asia. Quisumbing documentation: Seeds roasted and eaten (2-3 for children, 4-5 for adults) for intestinal worms — roundworm (Ascaris), threadworm (Enterobius). Leaves decocted for fever. Flowers for diarrhea. DOH registration 1997 for ascariasis (roundworm infection). Active compounds: Quisqualic acid (in seeds) — confirmed anthelmintic, excitotoxic glutamate agonist that causes roundworm paralysis. Validated mechanism: seeds cause flaccid paralysis of Ascaris — worms expelled passively, without dangerous purgation (advantage over traditional mercury-based anthelmintics). Safety concern: overdose causes hiccups. Traditional dosing protocol (2-3 seeds for children) reflects empirically calibrated therapeutic window. Convergence: multiple anthelmintic plants in this archive (epazote/paico, Quisqualis) demonstrate independent discovery of antiparasitic properties in plants from different plant families.

label: quisumbing::ampalaya_momordica
text: Ampalaya / Bitter Melon (Momordica charantia Linn.) — Family: Cucurbitaceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.559-562. Filipino names: Ampalaya (Tagalog); Paria (Ilocano, Bisaya); Amplaya (Pampanga). Distribution: Widely cultivated throughout Philippines. Quisumbing: Leaves and fruit for diabetes — 'boiled and eaten for sugar in urine.' Decoction of leaves for fever and as purgative. Juice for intestinal worms. Applied externally for skin conditions. Post-Quisumbing validation: one of the most extensively studied anti-diabetic plants in the world. Multiple Philippine clinical trials: PIMS trial (Philippine Institute of Complementary Medicine) showed significant HbA1c reduction over 3 months. Mechanisms: Charantin (steroidal compound) — insulin secretagogue; p-insulin (peptide) — structural similarity to bovine insulin; vicine — blocks complex I of mitochondrial electron transport affecting glucose metabolism. WHO traditional medicine guidelines cite ampalaya as having 'promising' anti-diabetic evidence. DOH Philippines registers ampalaya preparation for Type 2 diabetes adjunct treatment. One of the best-evidenced anti-diabetic plants globally — almost entirely unknown to mainstream endocrinology outside Asia.

label: quisumbing::guava_psidium
text: Bayabas / Guava (Psidium guajava Linn.) — Family: Myrtaceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.426-428. Filipino names: Bayabas (Tagalog); Biabas (Ilocano); Guayabas (Spanish-derived). Distribution: Widely cultivated throughout Philippines, pantropical. Quisumbing: Leaves boiled and used for wound washing — 'antibacterial wash' is the specific documented use. Bark for diarrhea. Fruit for constipation (unripe) and diarrhea (ripe). DOH Philippines: registered for wound and skin care antiseptic. Active research: Quercetin — major flavonoid, documented antimicrobial, antiviral (including HIV integrase inhibition in vitro), anti-inflammatory. Guava leaf extract active against Staphylococcus aureus, E. coli, Salmonella. Multiple clinical studies: guava leaf mouthwash — clinical RCT showing comparable efficacy to chlorhexidine for gingivitis. Guava leaf tea for diarrhea — clinical evidence. Convergence: guava used medicinally across pantropical distribution — Philippines, Brazil, India, West Africa — providing strong multi-tradition convergence signal for antimicrobial activity.

label: quisumbing::yerba_buena_mentha
text: Yerba Buena (Mentha cordifolia Opiz / M. arvensis) — Family: Lamiaceae. Quisumbing (1951) p.774-776. Filipino names: Yerba buena (Tagalog, from Spanish); Poleo (Ilocano); Hierba buena (Bisaya). Distribution: Throughout Philippines at medium to high elevation; cosmopolitan genus. Spanish influence visible in name — but documented uses are indigenous pre-dating Spanish contact in mainland Asia. Quisumbing: Leaves applied as poultice for headache, rheumatism, stomach pain. Juice drunk for stomach cramps, nausea. Menthol vapor inhaled for cough and colds. DOH Philippines: registered as herbal medicine for analgesic (pain relief) and for intestinal gas. Active compounds: Menthol (primary), menthone, pulegone, limonene. Menthol mechanisms: TRPM8 receptor agonist (cold sensation through calcium channel modulation), topical analgesic through counter-irritation. Convergence: Mentha used medicinally in every tradition in this archive that includes temperate plants — Greek/Roman (Dioscorides), Islamic (Ibn Sina), Chinese, Ayurvedic. Maximum global convergence for a single genus. The Philippine tradition's independent documentation of identical uses confirms the consistency of the global signal.
